title: Fitting an inline isolation valve
subtitle: Add a service valve to a copper pipe for future appliance isolation
excerpt: How to cut into an existing 15 mm copper pipe and fit a compression-end isolation valve so that an appliance, tap, or toilet cistern can be isolated without turning off the mains supply, including draining the pipe section, making the compression connections, and testing the valve.
sourceNotes: Collins Complete DIY Manual. Pegler Yorkshire and Hep2O installation guides. CIPHE guidance on domestic service valves.

glossaryTerms.definition: A quarter-turn flat-head screw valve fitted in a pipe run. The valve is open when the flat-blade screw slot is in line with the pipe and closed when it is crossways. The flat-blade screw slot allows the valve to be operated with a screwdriver rather than a spanner, which is useful in confined spaces such as below a sink or behind a cistern.

A small brass ring that sits over the pipe end between the fitting body and compression nut. As the nut is tightened, it compresses the olive onto the pipe, creating a watertight seal. Olives are supplied with compression fittings; always use a new olive — a previously compressed olive will not form a reliable seal.

A compact mini pipe cutter designed for cutting copper pipe in confined spaces where a rotating full-size cutter cannot be used. The slice is pushed onto the pipe and rotated by hand until the pipe is severed. Available in 15 mm and 22 mm sizes.

content.text: An
isolation valve

is fitted in the supply pipe immediately upstream of a tap, appliance, or cistern so that the appliance can be serviced or replaced without shutting off the whole house supply. This is one of the most useful preventive plumbing tasks to carry out. The job requires cutting the live pipe after isolating upstream: confirm the upstream isolation point works before making the cut.
Step 1, isolate and drain the pipe

Close the nearest upstream valve (gate valve, stopcock, or mains stopcock). Open the downstream tap or flush the cistern to drain water from the section. Have a cloth or tray ready: the cut pipe will release residual water even after draining. Confirm the pipe is empty before cutting.
Step 2, cut out a section of pipe

Mark the position for the isolation valve: it must be accessible for future operation, so avoid cutting into a section that will be boxed in. Measure the valve body length and add 6 mm each side for the compression sockets. Cut two sections from the existing pipe at these positions using the pipe cutter or
pipe slice
in tight spaces. Remove the cut-out section. Deburr both pipe ends with the deburring tool.
Step 3, prepare the pipe ends
Polish both cut pipe ends with the emery cloth to bright copper: a dull or oxidised surface prevents the
olive

seating correctly. The pipe end must be round: if slightly oval from the cutter, true it up with the deburring pin. Slide a compression nut and then a new olive over each pipe end before inserting into the valve body.
Step 4, fit the isolation valve

Check the flow direction arrow on the valve body and ensure it aligns with the direction of water flow in the pipe. Insert one pipe end into the valve compression socket until it meets the internal stop. Slide the olive to the fitting and thread on the compression nut. Hand-tighten the nut, then tighten with the adjustable spanner one full turn past hand-tight. Repeat for the other end. Set the
isolation valve
screw to the closed position (slot crossways to the pipe) before restoring the supply.
Step 5, test both positions

Restore the upstream supply. Watch both compression joints for drips for 2 minutes. If neither joint weeps, open the isolation valve (slot in line with pipe) and confirm the downstream supply flows normally. Then close the isolation valve (slot crossways) and confirm flow is stopped. The valve should operate with a small flat-blade screwdriver without effort.
Variations

Angled isolation valves (inlet and outlet at 90°) are used below a basin or kitchen tap where the pipe rises vertically and the tap tail drops vertically. They are fitted in the same way as inline valves: only the body orientation differs. Lockable isolation valves are available where unauthorised tampering is a concern.

attrs.symptom: Compression joint weeps at one or both ends
attrs.cause: Olive not fully seated; pipe end not inserted to the internal stop; joint overtightened so olive has sheared rather than compressed evenly.
attrs.fix: Isolate, drain, dismantle the joint, fit a new olive, and retighten to one full turn past hand-tight.

attrs.symptom: Valve does not fully stop the flow when closed
attrs.cause: Valve installed backwards (against the flow arrow); debris has jammed the ball.
attrs.fix: Isolate, drain, and remove the valve. Flush the pipe section. Refit with attention to the flow direction arrow. If the ball is jammed with scale, replace the valve.

attrs.symptom: There is insufficient straight pipe to fit the valve
attrs.cause: An elbow or tee fitting is too close to the cut position; pipe run too short between two bends.
attrs.fix: Use an angled isolation valve. If no angled valve will fit, consider moving the cut position to a longer run of straight pipe further upstream.
